name: The Lighthouse
image_prompt: Interior ground floor of a decommissioned stone lighthouse on a rocky Irish island, dusk during a gale. Thick round stone walls three feet thick, small deep-set windows with leaded glass. A large stone hearth taking up a quarter of one wall with a peat fire burning orange. An iron spiral staircase rising through the ceiling, candles in an iron chandelier overhead. A heavy oak table, a pair of worn leather chairs facing the fire, a bottle of whiskey and glasses on the table. Rain lashing the windows. Glimpse of stormy Atlantic cliffs through the nearest window. Empty, no people. Colors: weathered gray stone, firelight orange, deep blue storm light, warm candle amber. Moody Celtic atmosphere.
location: A decommissioned lighthouse on Inishmore, the largest of the Aran Islands off the west coast of Ireland. Stone walls three feet thick. The client's family has owned it since 1891. The last keeper left in 1978 and nobody changed the locks.
time: 6 PM, early March. The sun sets in forty minutes. The light will not come back for fourteen hours.
weather: Atlantic gale building from the southwest. Rain that tastes like salt. The wind sounds different at each of the four windows because they face different headlands. The sky is the color of wet wool.

atmosphere.lighting: A single iron chandelier fitted with candles because the wiring was pulled in the nineties. Firelight from a stone hearth that takes up a quarter of the ground floor wall. The old lamp room above glows faintly from a battery lantern someone left on the spiral stair.
atmosphere.sound: Wind against stone, the Atlantic breaking on cliffs eighty feet below, fire cracking, rain on thick glass, the groan of the spiral staircase when anyone moves above, a loose shutter on the north side that bangs once every few minutes like punctuation
atmosphere.crowd: A working group in a room built for two. The ground floor is tight. Elbows touch. Every conversation is heard by everyone. There is nowhere to hide except up the spiral stairs into the lamp room, and the lamp room has glass walls.
atmosphere.smell: Peat smoke, wet wool, salt air that gets through every crack, old stone, the waxy sweetness of candles, strong tea, someone opened a bottle of Redbreast and the whiskey smell filled the room in seconds

layout.hearth: The stone fireplace dominates the west wall. A peat fire throws orange light and moving shadows. Two high-backed wooden chairs flank it. One holds a mug of tea catching the firelight in fogged glasses. The other holds a patient watcher, gold bangle lit amber, waiting on the flames with the confidence of someone who knows the answer is in the waiting.
layout.central_table: A round oak table scarred by a century of keepers' knives and teacups. Smaller than the gathering it serves. Notebooks and mugs crowd the surface. Papers overlap. An elbow rests on a diagram and neither owner has mentioned it.
layout.window_seats: Deep stone alcoves at each of the four windows, cushioned with wool blankets. Someone sits in the south window watching the storm build, knees drawn up. Another sits in the east window cataloging lichen on the stone sill. A third leans in the north window looking at the mainland, barely visible through rain.
layout.spiral_stair: A tight iron staircase spiraling up through the ceiling to the lamp room. Someone sits on the third step, above the group but still in earshot. Someone else stands at the base, one hand on the railing, the other holding coffee, glancing upward as if the answer might be at the top.
layout.lamp_room: The glass-walled room at the top where the light used to be. The old Fresnel lens was removed decades ago. Now it is an empty glass cylinder open to the storm on all sides. A silhouette visible from below turns slowly against the gray sky, mapping something nobody else can see.

objects_of_note: The keeper's logbook from 1943 to 1978, left on a shelf by the door. The last entry reads 'Light operational. Sea state 4. No vessels. Closing this book.' Someone read the entire thing before the others arrived.
A brass barometer mounted by the door. The needle has been falling since morning. It gets checked every thirty minutes.
The problem brief in a waxed canvas envelope on the round table, held down by a smooth stone from the beach placed with deliberate care.
Mismatched mugs from a cupboard that has not been opened in years. Each one is chipped. Someone chose the least damaged. Someone else chose the most.
A telescope in the lamp room pointed at nothing. Someone adjusted it once and walked away.
mood: Compressed. The lighthouse forces closeness. A working group in a stone cylinder with fire and wind and no room for distance. Every word carries. Every silence is noticed. The storm outside makes the walls feel like protection and prison at the same time. The spiral stair offers a vertical escape that only leads to more exposure. The fire is the only warmth and everyone angles toward it. By morning the problem will be solved or the room will have exhausted itself. The lighthouse does not care which.
